ENABLING UNIFIED CLINICAL EDUCATION OPERATIONS 
THROUGH AN INTEGRATED DIGITAL PLATFORM  
CHALLENGE OWNER 
The National University Hospital (NUH) is a leading healthcare institution in Singapore and the largest 
medical center within the National University Health System (NUHS). Established in 1985, NUH serves 
over one million patients annually with its 1,200 be ds and offers a comprehensive range of medical, 
surgical, and dental specialties. As an academic medical centr e, it functions as the principal teaching 
hospital for the NUS Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, actively engaging in research and international 
collaborations. NUH is providing integrated care for adults, women, and children, including specialised 
services like the country's sole pediatric kidney and liver transplant program.  With state-of-the-art 
facilities and a dedicated team of healthcare professionals, NUH continues to lead in patient care,

metadata.creator: Microsoft Word
metadata.author: Mun choong Ho
metadata.source: C:\SlashLLM\clinedops\DOCS\Challenge Brief_NUH (Medical Affairs)_Unified clinical education ops through an integrated digital platform_final.pdf

page_content:
facilities and a dedicated team of healthcare professionals, NUH continues to lead in patient care, 
medical education, and research, playing a crucial role in advancing Singapore's healthcare landscape. 
 
CONTEXT 
NUH delivers clinical education across Medicine, Allied Health, Nursing and other training 
programmes. While each discipline has distinct operational nuances, they share common core 
activities which are student onboarding and postings management, tutor eng agement, teaching 
activity tracking, feedback collection and reporting. The Medical Affairs (Education) department at 
NUH plays a central coordinating role in delivering structured clinical education for medical, nursing 
and allied health students across multiple partner institutions such as SIT, NUS, Singapore Polytechnic 
and PSB Academy. Beyond facilitating student postings, the department manages teaching schedules, 
attendance tracking, assessment and feedback records, ensuring that data flows into downstream

metadata.creator: Microsoft Word
metadata.author: Mun choong Ho
metadata.source: C:\SlashLLM\clinedops\DOCS\Challenge Brief_NUH (Medical Affairs)_Unified clinical education ops through an integrated digital platform_final.pdf

page_content:
and PSB Academy. Beyond facilitating student postings, the department manages teaching schedules, 
attendance tracking, assessment and feedback records, ensuring that data flows into downstream 
processes such as faculty appraisal, teaching awards, tutor billing and continuous programme quality 
improvement. As clinical education expands in scale and complexity, the department’s operational 
responsibilities have grown significantly, requiring tighter coordination across disciplines, 
departments and institutions. 
 
During clinical postings, students interact with multiple tutors across specialties, each contributing 
supervision hours and providing formative and summative feedback. Administrative teams must 
ensure that teaching activities and feedback are accurately c aptured, matched to the correct  
student-tutor pairings, and consolidated in a timely manner. This data supports critical downstream

metadata.creator: Microsoft Word
metadata.author: Mun choong Ho
metadata.source: C:\SlashLLM\clinedops\DOCS\Challenge Brief_NUH (Medical Affairs)_Unified clinical education ops through an integrated digital platform_final.pdf

page_content:
ensure that teaching activities and feedback are accurately c aptured, matched to the correct  
student-tutor pairings, and consolidated in a timely manner. This data supports critical downstream 
processes including student feedback summaries, faculty appraisal, teaching awards, tutor billing, 
accreditation reporting and continuous quality improvement initiatives.  However, data management 
practices differ significantly across disciplines.  
 
For Allied Health, Nursing and Training programmes, there is currently no dedicated digital 
management system. Administrative teams manage the full student lifecycle, from onboarding to 
offboarding, largely through manual Excel trackers and FormSG submissi ons. Midpoint feedback is 
often conducted through face -to-face check -ins, while end -of-posting evaluations are collected 
separately. Although workflows are broadly similar across programmes, small operational differences

page_content:
often conducted through face -to-face check -ins, while end -of-posting evaluations are collected 
separately. Although workflows are broadly similar across programmes, small operational differences 
require additional manual handling.  This fragmented approa ch has resulted in duplicated effort, 
inconsistent data standards, missed submissions, incorrect student -tutor matching, reporting delays 
and increased risk of errors. 
 
For the Medical discipline, feedback and teaching data are collected through more structured systems. 
However, these systems remain fragmented and lack interoperability and flexibility. Managing large 
student cohorts, multiple rotations and frequent academ ic year transitions still requires substantial

manual coordination. Data inconsistencies across postings and departments remain a persistent 
challenge, limited transparency and efficiency. 
Across all disciplines, the manual and fragmented processes create several challenges for NUH: 
• Fragmented student lifecycle management. There is no integrated system to manage end-to-
end pre-boarding, onboarding and offboarding processes, resulting in duplicated effort or 
higher risk of administrative errors.  
• Disconnected and manual data management. Teaching and feedback data are stored across 
multiple unlinked platforms, requiring manual consolidation and reducing data accuracy and 
reliability. 
• Inaccurate tracking of teaching hours.  Teaching hours are manually logged or optionally 
submitted by students, leading to low compliance and inaccurate records. Event -by-event 
tagging of tutor and student interactions is also time-consuming.

metadata.creator: Microsoft Word
metadata.author: Mun choong Ho
metadata.source: C:\SlashLLM\clinedops\DOCS\Challenge Brief_NUH (Medical Affairs)_Unified clinical education ops through an integrated digital platform_final.pdf

page_content:
submitted by students, leading to low compliance and inaccurate records. Event -by-event 
tagging of tutor and student interactions is also time-consuming.  
• Inefficient feedback monitoring. Feedback collection lacks automation and real-time visibility, 
resulting in missed submissions, compliance gaps and delayed follow -ups. Administrators 
must also manually track feedback completion and send reminders to students.  
• Reporting and analytics bottlenecks.  Manual report generation and limited customisation 
capabilities create heavy administrative workload and constrain timely, data-driven decision-
making.  
• Survey overload and fatigue. During clinical postings, students interact with multiple tutors 
across specialties. S urveys currently are being sent for every tutor and student interaction 
regardless of how many sessions the student goes through in a single day. This results in 
survey fatigue and low participation rate amongst students.

metadata.creator: Microsoft Word
metadata.author: Mun choong Ho
metadata.source: C:\SlashLLM\clinedops\DOCS\Challenge Brief_NUH (Medical Affairs)_Unified clinical education ops through an integrated digital platform_final.pdf

page_content:
regardless of how many sessions the student goes through in a single day. This results in 
survey fatigue and low participation rate amongst students.   
 
Several solutions have been trialled by NUH, including QR code-based surveys for collecting teaching 
hours and feedback, Microsoft Power Apps for structured data capture and the upgrade of internal 
system to the BLUE system (a shared system extended by NUS to NUH). However, these approaches 
did not resolve the core challenges as they were piecemeal enhancements that did not sufficiently 
streamline and integrate workflows, standardise data or provide a single, reliable source of truth 
across disciplines.  
 
PROBLEM STATEMENT 
How might we streamline and standardise fragmented clinical education workflows through a single, 
unified system that improves operational efficiency and reduces manual effort across disciplines? 
 
WHAT ARE WE LOOKING FOR?

metadata.creator: Microsoft Word
metadata.author: Mun choong Ho
metadata.source: C:\SlashLLM\clinedops\DOCS\Challenge Brief_NUH (Medical Affairs)_Unified clinical education ops through an integrated digital platform_final.pdf

page_content:
unified system that improves operational efficiency and reduces manual effort across disciplines? 
 
WHAT ARE WE LOOKING FOR?  
Manual consolidation remains common across the various NUH disciplines, increasing administrative 
workload and slowing decision -making for appraisal, tutor billing, awards and programme quality 
improvement. As clinical education volume grow and stakeholder expectations increase, there is a 
pressing need to move towards a consolidated, scalable system that can harmonise workflows, 
standardise data collection and provide a single source of truth across Medicine, Allied Health, Nursing 
and Training programmes.  
 
The solution should meet the following criteria:  
• Student and tutor lifecycle management.  Centralise the management of student and tutor 
records across Medicine, Allied Health, Nursing and Training programmes. The solution should 
have the ability to assign student to departments, posting, tutors and academic cycles. There

records across Medicine, Allied Health, Nursing and Training programmes. The solution should 
have the ability to assign student to departments, posting, tutors and academic cycles. There 
should be clear status tracking for onboarding, active and completed postings. 
• Tracking of teaching hours. Provide a standardised and validated mechanism for capturing and 
verifying teaching contributions with the ability to tag sessions to specific students, posting

and departments. The solution should be able to allow tagging of approved hours to billable 
rates for compensation processing. 
• Configurable survey templates.  Allow configurable survey templates by discipline, posting 
type or semester, with the scoring m atrix based out of 5 . The solution should also have the 
ability to include compulsory text fields to capture reasons for surveys that receive low scores 
and flag them to the relevant users.  
• Structured feedback collection.  Standardise and streamline qualitative and quantitative 
feedback processes including real-time visibility of submission rates, automated reminders for 
incomplete submissions, tools to filter or categorise positive and negative feedback and the 
ability to g enerate customisable reports for supervisors, tutors, institutions for awards or 
appraisal purposes.  
• Notifications for pending and incomplete tasks. Trigger alerts or notifications to the relevant

appraisal purposes.  
• Notifications for pending and incomplete tasks. Trigger alerts or notifications to the relevant 
users for tasks that are pending completion or approvals.   
• Batch upload of student and tutor data.  Support the batch upload of student and tutor data 
(i.e. from FormSG and Microsoft Excel) in each academic cycle with the ability to detect 
duplicated entries, errors and anomalies.  
• Role-based access control. Must provide robust governance controls, with NUH as the primary 
owner. This includes role -based access controls (e.g. super admin, programme admin, 
supervisor, tutor), customisable permissions and approval hierarchies for handling the 
different approval workflows and viewership rights.  
• Real-time dashboard and reporting capabilities.  Provide a real -time dashboard of teaching 
hours by tutor, department and discipline  as well as survey completion rates and deadlines .

• Real-time dashboard and reporting capabilities.  Provide a real -time dashboard of teaching 
hours by tutor, department and discipline  as well as survey completion rates and deadlines . 
The solution should be capable of generating reports to support faculty appraisal, billing, 
awards and programme quality improvement. 
• Maintenance of audit trail.  Full audit trail of submissions, edits and approvals should be 
captured with timestamps for audit and compliance purposes.  
• Data exportability function.  Ability for administrators to download and export reports for 
finance, appraisal and accreditation purposes.  
• Interface with existing information system.  Ability to integrate with the existing hospital 
systems (e.g. NUHS Jobplan Portal/ NUS BLUE/ Teaching Activities System (TAS) ) to ensure 
data completeness and seamless data flow, as part of the eventual deployment beyond the 
prototype/POC.  
 
OVERALL PERFORMANCE REQUIREMENTS

metadata.author: Mun choong Ho

data completeness and seamless data flow, as part of the eventual deployment beyond the 
prototype/POC.  
 
OVERALL PERFORMANCE REQUIREMENTS 
• Accurate and efficient.  Demonstrate accuracy in tracking teaching hours, reduce manual 
transcription and report generation time.   
• User-friendly. Intuitive interface for clinicians, students and administrators. 
• Configurable without heavy custom coding. Ability to adapt to different disciplines and future 
workflow changes without the need for heavy custom coding. 
• Scalable. The solution should be scalable to handle large cohorts , growing student numbers, 
additional programmes and multi-institution data.  
• Security and data privacy. Ensure that all staff login are secured and with access logs. All data 
should also be secured and comply with local data protection regulations (e.g. PDPA).  
• Hosting on local cloud.  The solution  must be deployed in a Singapore -based cloud

• Hosting on local cloud.  The solution  must be deployed in a Singapore -based cloud 
environment to meet local regulations and data residency requirements.  
 
There are no restrictions on the geographical location of the problem solvers who may choose to apply 
to this challenge. However, the prototype must be demonstrated in Singapore.

METRICS OF SUCCESS  
• Improved operation efficiency. Achieve a 50% reduction in time spent on manual data entry 
and tracking of teaching hours. 
• Improved data accuracy . Achieve at least 90% data accuracy and completeness by 
consolidating and standardising data across all disciplines.  
• Faster report generation rates. Reduction in report generation time by at least 80%, from the 
current five minutes to below one minute for appraisal, billing, awards and programme quality 
improvement purposes. 
• Improved staff and user satisfaction rates. With the implementation of the solution, internal 
staff (administrators, tutors, clinicians) and user (students) satisfaction should increase, with 
at least a 30% increase in survey completion rates. 
 
POSSIBLE USE CASES 
1. Improved teaching hours and feedback consolidation . Sarah, who is with the Allied Health

POSSIBLE USE CASES 
1. Improved teaching hours and feedback consolidation . Sarah, who is with the Allied Health 
Education team , coordinates clinical postings for physiotherapy, occupational therapy and 
speech therapy students across multiple institutions. She manages onboarding, tracks teaching 
hours, collects feedback, prepares tutor evaluation reports and processes billing data.  She 
maintains multiple Excel spreadsheets to track students, tutors and teaching hours. Feedback is 
collected via FormSG, which she manually downloads, filters and reformats into reports. She 
toggles between CSV files, emails and PDFs to reconcile records . Teaching hours are sometimes 
submitted late or inconsistently, and she has no easy way to verify accuracy. During peak periods, 
she spends days consolidating reports for tutor appraisals and awards.

submitted late or inconsistently, and she has no easy way to verify accuracy. During peak periods, 
she spends days consolidating reports for tutor appraisals and awards. 
 
With an integrated platform, Sarah can view all student postings, tutor assignments, teaching 
hours and feedback submissions via a single dashboard. Teaching hours are logged directly by 
tutors and routed for approval, with automated anomaly flags. Feedback data is also 
automatically aggregated and report -ready. Instead of manual reconciliation, Sarah generates 
customised reports with one click, significantly reducing ad ministrative workload and improving 
accuracy. 
 
2. Fuss-free logging of teaching hours for medical tutors.  Dr Aisha supervises medical students 
across different rotations, conducts formal teaching sessions and provides ad hoc consultations. 
She is required to log teaching hours and review student feedback. Teaching hours are currently

across different rotations, conducts formal teaching sessions and provides ad hoc consultations. 
She is required to log teaching hours and review student feedback. Teaching hours are currently 
logged through a cumberso me system, and additional hours must sometimes be submitted via 
email. Students optionally input their hours, leading to discrepancies. Dr Aisha has limited 
visibility of whether her hours have been ap proved or accurately recorded. Reviewing feedback 
requires navigating multiple screens and there is no consolidated view of her teaching 
contributions. 
 
Through a user -friendly interface, Dr Aisha logs both scheduled and ad hoc sessions within 
minutes. She can see her accumulated teaching hours in real time and track approval status. 
Feedback summaries are automatically compiled into a dashboard view, allowing her to quickly 
identify strengths and areas for improvement without administrative friction.

3. Better quality monitoring and strategic oversight.  Bryan, the head of Medical Affairs , is 
responsible for overseeing clinical education across Medicine, Allied Health, Nursing and Training 
programmes. He oversees job shadowing applications, shortlists candidates, assigns clinician 
mentors and ensures teaching quality standards are met. He a lso tracks positive feedback for 
tutor recognition and identifies improvement opportunities from negative feedback. Student 
applications are reviewed manually and mentor -student matching is tracked in spreadsheets.

Positive feedback must be reformatted for award nominations, while negative feedback is 
manually extracted for programme review discussions. Data across discipline is fragmented, 
making it difficult to obtain a consolidated view of teaching contributions, feedback trends and 
compliance rates. Strategic decisions rely heavily on manually compiled reports that may already 
be outdated.  
 
The solution provides  Bryan with access to  cross-disciplinary dashboards and standardised 
reporting. He gains real-time visibility into teaching hours, feedback completion rates and quality 
indicators. With reliable data at his fingertips, he can make informed decisions, strengthen 
governance and better support long-term programme improvement.

subnet

subnet
 •  FastAPI  →  RDS  PostgreSQL  (Multi-AZ,  private  subnet)  •  FastAPI  →  ElastiCache  Redis  (Celery  broker  +  caching)  •  Celery  workers  (separate  ECS  task)  →  AWS  SES  (emails)  +  S3  (file  storage)  •  All  ECS  tasks  in  VPC  private  subnets;  ALB  in  public  subnet  •  AWS  WAF  on  ALB  for  rate  limiting  and  injection  protection  •  CloudWatch  for  logs,  metrics,  alarms;  Sentry  SDK  in  both  frontend  and  backend

6.  API  Specification   
6.1  API  Design  Principles  •  Base  URL:  /api/v1/  •  All  responses:  JSON  with  standard  envelope:  {  data,  meta,  errors  }  •  Authentication:  Bearer  JWT  token  in  Authorization  header  •  Pagination:  cursor-based  for  large  datasets;  limit/offset  for  simple  lists  •  Versioning:  URL  path  versioning  (/api/v1/,  /api/v2/)  •  Error  format:  {  code,  message,  field  (if  validation),  trace_id  }  •  Rate  limiting:  1000  req/min  per  authenticated  user;  100  req/min  for  public  endpoints

# ClinEdOps — Live Demo Talking Script  
**Aligned with:** NUH Medical Affairs (Education) — *Unified clinical education ops through an integrated digital platform* (Call 28, March 2026)

Use this script as a **guide**, not a rigid teleprompter. Pause for questions where marked. Adjust depth to your audience (executives vs. operations vs. IT).

---

## Before you go live (30 seconds, internal)

- Log in as **super_admin** or **programme_admin** for the “operations” arc; keep a **tutor** and **supervisor** account ready for quick role switches (or two browser profiles).
- Seed or prep data so **at least one posting**, **teaching session**, and **survey** path is visible—empty states kill momentum.
- Hide personal data; use demo emails and synthetic names.

---

## Opening hook (1–2 min)

**Say:**

> “NUH delivers clinical education across Medicine, Allied Health, Nursing, and training programmes. The brief puts it plainly: the **same core work**—onboarding, postings, tutor engagement, teaching hours, feedback, and reporting—has been happening in **parallel silos**: Excel, FormSG, different systems for medicine versus everyone else.  
> 
> That creates pain we can all feel: **duplicated effort**, **missed submissions**, **wrong student–tutor matching**, **reporting delays**, and **survey fatigue** when students get pinged after every micro-interaction.  
> 
> **ClinEdOps** is our answer to the challenge question: *How might we streamline and standardise fragmented workflows through a single system?*  
> 
> In the next [X] minutes I’ll show you **one platform**, **one source of truth**, and **role-based views** so Sarah in Allied Health ops, Dr Aisha on the ward, and Bryan in Medical Affairs leadership each get what they need—without reconciling CSVs on a Sunday night.”

**Optional audience question:**

> “Who here has ever merged two spreadsheets and prayed they were the same version?” *[light laugh, hands]*

---

## Set expectations (30 sec)

**Say:**

> “I’ll move in **user story order**: first how we **run the programme**—students, tutors, postings—then **teaching hours** and **approvals**, then **feedback and surveys**, then **reporting and governance**. Stop me anytime.”

---

## Flow 1 — One lifecycle, four disciplines (3–4 min)  
**Pain point addressed:** *Fragmented student lifecycle; no integrated pre-boarding → active → completed view.*

**Navigate:** Dashboard → **Students** / **Tutors** / **Postings** / **Departments** / **Academic cycles** (as your build exposes).

**Say:**

**Navigate:** Dashboard → **Students** / **Tutors** / **Postings** / **Departments** / **Academic cycles** (as your build exposes).

**Say:**

> “The brief asks for **centralised** student and tutor records across Medicine, Allied Health, Nursing, and training— with **clear status** for onboarding, active posting, and completion. Here, discipline isn’t an afterthought; it’s a **first-class filter**.  
> 
> Instead of one tracker per programme, we have **one directory** with the relationships the hospital actually uses: who is on which **posting**, which **department**, which **cycle**. That directly attacks **incorrect matching** and **inconsistent data standards**.”

**Demo beats:**

1. Open **Students** (or **Tutors**) — show list with discipline / status.  
   *“This is where ‘Sarah’ stops maintaining three Excel masters.”*

**Demo beats:**

1. Open **Students** (or **Tutors**) — show list with discipline / status.  
   *“This is where ‘Sarah’ stops maintaining three Excel masters.”*

2. Open a **posting** or student detail — show link to tutors / department / cycle.  
   *“The posting is the hub—the brief’s requirement to assign students to departments, postings, tutors, and academic cycles.”*

**Transition:**

> “Once people and placements are real in the system, **teaching** and **feedback** can attach to the **right pairing**—not to a loose email thread.”

---

## Flow 2 — Teaching hours: tutor-first, structured (3–4 min)  
**Pain point addressed:** *Inaccurate hours; low compliance when only students log; cumbersome logging; limited visibility of approval status.*  
**Persona:** *Dr Aisha — fuss-free logging.*

**Navigate:** Switch to **tutor** (or stay admin and narrate tutor view) → **Teaching sessions** → **Log** / create session.

**Say:**

**Navigate:** Switch to **tutor** (or stay admin and narrate tutor view) → **Teaching sessions** → **Log** / create session.

**Say:**

> “The challenge calls out a vicious cycle: hours are **manual**, sometimes **student-submitted**, so compliance is low and records drift. ClinEdOps standardises capture so **tutors log** structured sessions—scheduled, ad hoc, consultation—with **students and postings** tagged.  
> 
> Dr Aisha shouldn’t need a second job as data entry. If she can log a session in **minutes** and see it move through **approval**, we’ve removed the ‘email the medical education office’ detour.”

**Demo beats:**

1. **Create or open a teaching session** — show type, time, linkage to posting/students.  
2. **Submit for approval** (if applicable) — *“Status is visible; no black hole.”*  
3. Mention **billing hours** if in scope — *“Approved hours can align with billable constructs—the brief’s link to compensation processing.”*

**Transition:**

**Transition:**

> “Logging is only half the trust story. Someone has to **say yes**—that’s governance, not bureaucracy.”

## Flow 3 — Approvals and discipline scope (2–3 min)  
**Pain point addressed:** *Data accuracy and governance; RBAC and approval hierarchies.*

**Navigate:** **Supervisor** or **programme admin** → teaching session **approve / reject** (or equivalent).

**Say:**

> “The brief is explicit: **role-based access** and **approval workflows**. Supervisors and programme admins see what they’re allowed to see—typically **their discipline**—and can **approve or reject** with audit-friendly outcomes.  
> 
> That’s how we move from ‘trust the spreadsheet’ to **defensible records** for appraisal and billing.”

**Demo beats:**

1. Show **approve/reject** on a pending session.  
2. One line on **discipline scoping** — *“A nursing programme admin isn’t editing medicine’s cohort by accident.”*

## Flow 4 — Surveys & feedback: templates, completion, fatigue (4–5 min)  
**Pain point addressed:** *Survey overload; inefficient monitoring; low scores and compulsory text; strategic visibility.*

**Navigate:** **Survey templates** → **Pending surveys** / student **fill** path → **Survey analytics** or tutor-facing feedback view.

**Say:**

**Navigate:** **Survey templates** → **Pending surveys** / student **fill** path → **Survey analytics** or tutor-facing feedback view.

**Say:**

> “Two problems in one paragraph in the brief: first, **survey fatigue** from firing a survey after every tiny touch; second, **no real-time visibility** of who’s done what, so admins chase people manually.  
> 
> ClinEdOps supports **configurable templates**—by discipline and context—with **structured scoring** and room for **mandatory comments when scores are low**, so red flags aren’t buried in averages. Pair that with **notifications and reminders** for pending work, and you’ve automated the nagging without losing the humanity.”

# RBAC Mapping Matrix

This document defines the expected access control for each role in ClinEdOps.

## Role Definitions
- **Super Admin (`super_admin`)**: Global access to all disciplines and system administration.
- **Program Admin (`programme_admin`)**: Administrative access scoped to a specific discipline (e.g., Medicine).
- **Supervisor / HOD (`supervisor`)**: Read-only access to discipline data + Approval rights for teaching sessions.
- **Tutor (`tutor`)**: Logs teaching hours and views self-analytics.
- **Student (`student`)**: Logs teaching hours and completes surveys.

## Discipline Scoping Rules
- If `actor.role != super_admin`, then `actor.discipline` MUST match the discipline of the target entity (Student, Tutor, Session, etc.).
- Access is denied (403) if the discipline scope is violated.
